contents.title: ## Introduction
contents.text:
This report summarizes the results of public input, encompassing:
 * __20 statements__
 * __64 votes__
 * 4 topics
 * 11 subtopics

All voters were anonymous.

contents.title: ## Overview
contents.text:
Below is a high level overview of the topics discussed in the conversation, as well as the percentage of statements categorized under each topic. Note that the percentages may add up to greater than 100% when statements fall under more than one topic.

* **Human-centered AI in care (40%):** Statements call for AI to augment staff time and provide companionship without replacing human contact, while creating local jobs and prioritizing community well‑being over cutting‑edge technology. They stress that imperfect AI tools can still aid families and students when real‑person oversight is maintained.
* **Transparency and accountability in AI decisions (25%):** Participants warn of harm from opaque AI decisions, demand clear labeling of non‑human agents, and call for compensation to creators whose data trains systems.
* **Workforce impact of AI automation (20%):** Statements urge concrete employment assistance for workers displaced by AI and a pause on deployment until its effects are fully understood.
* **Community involvement in AI governance (15%):** Participants insist that people with dementia and local communities must have a say in using AI tools, framing participation as essential authority over AI use in care and education.

contents.title: ## Top 5 Most Discussed Subtopics
contents.text: 11 subtopics of discussion emerged. These 5 subtopics had the most statements submitted.

subContents.title: Prominent themes were:
subContents.text:
- AI frees staff to engage residents  
- AI provides comfort when humans absent  
- Local jobs boost community wellbeing  
- Community care outweighs advanced tech  
- Human interaction remains essential

subContents.title: Prominent themes were:
subContents.text:
- AI assists caregivers despite flaws  
- Tools empower families in illness care  
- Schools leverage AI to aid learning  
- Imperfect tech still provides value  
- Community should guide AI use

subContents.title: ### 3. Balancing technology with community values (2 statements)
Prominent themes were:
subContents.text:
- Human oversight essential  
- AI transparency required  
- Community involvement matters  
- Education fosters critical thinking  
- Access to human support

subContents.title: ### 4. Job loss due to automation (2 statements)
Prominent themes were:
subContents.text:
- Job loss from automated systems  
- Workers need real job assistance  
- Pause AI until human impact clear  
- Decide who controls AI use

subContents.title: Prominent themes were:
subContents.text:
- AI care decisions include people  
- Community voice in AI use  
- Dementia patients' input matters  
- Local schools and hospitals  
- Participatory decision-making needed

contents.title: ## Topics
contents.text: From the statements submitted, 4 high level topics were identifiedas well as 11 subtopics. Based on voting patterns both points of common ground as well as differences of opinion have been identified and are described below.

subContents.title: ### Human-centered AI in care (8 statements)
subContents.text: This topic included 3 subtopics, comprising a total of 8 statements.

subContents.title: #### Preserving human interaction (4 statements)
subContents.text: This subtopic had high alignment compared to the other subtopics.

subContents.title: Prominent themes were:
subContents.text:
* **Human Interaction Preservation in Care Settings**: AI should augment staff time with residents rather than supplanting direct contact.  
* **AI Companionship as a Safety Net**: Using AI to provide companionship when no human is present is preferable to isolation.  
* **Local Economic Benefits of AI Infrastructure**: New data centres should generate jobs locally, not just attract remote tech workers.  
* **Community Well‑Being Over Technological Advancement**: A community’s care for its people outweighs having the most advanced technology.

subContents.title: Common ground:
subContents.text: No statements met the thresholds necessary to be considered as a point of common ground (at least 20 votes, and at least 70% agreement).

subContents.title: Differences of opinion:
subContents.text: No statements met the thresholds necessary to be considered as a significant difference of opinion (at least 20 votes, and more than 40%% and 60%% difference in agreement rate between groups).

subContents.title: #### Supporting caregivers and families (2 statements)
subContents.text: This subtopic had high alignment compared to the other subtopics.

subContents.title: Prominent themes were:
subContents.text:
* **Imperfect AI Support**: Offering imperfect AI tools can still provide meaningful assistance to families caring for seriously ill individuals.  
* **AI Educational Benefits**: AI tools in schools primarily benefit struggling students by helping them catch up.

subContents.title: Common ground:
subContents.text: No statements met the thresholds necessary to be considered as a point of common ground (at least 20 votes, and at least 70% agreement).

subContents.title: Differences of opinion:
subContents.text: No statements met the thresholds necessary to be considered as a significant difference of opinion (at least 20 votes, and more than 40%% and 60%% difference in agreement rate between groups).

subContents.title: #### Balancing technology with community values (2 statements)
subContents.text: This subtopic had high alignment compared to the other subtopics.

subContents.title: Prominent themes were:
subContents.text:
* **Human Oversight Required**: The statement emphasizes that citizens must retain access to a real person when interacting with government services, even if AI is used.  
* **Critical Thinking Education**: The statement emphasizes that schools must teach children to critically evaluate AI information rather than merely using it.

subContents.title: Common ground:
subContents.text: No statements met the thresholds necessary to be considered as a point of common ground (at least 20 votes, and at least 70% agreement).

subContents.title: Differences of opinion:
subContents.text: No statements met the thresholds necessary to be considered as a significant difference of opinion (at least 20 votes, and more than 40%% and 60%% difference in agreement rate between groups).

subContents.title: ### Transparency and accountability in AI decisions (5 statements)
subContents.text: This topic included 1 subtopic, comprising a total of 5 statements.

subContents.title: #### Other (3 statements)
subContents.text: This subtopic had high alignment compared to the other subtopics.

subContents.title: Prominent themes were:
subContents.text:
* **AI Decisional Harm**: People are being harmed by AI-driven decisions while the government takes too long to act.  
* **Transparency of AI Identity**: When I talk to a chatbot or AI assistant, I should always be told it is not a real person.  
* **Compensation for Training Data**: AI companies should have to pay artists and writers when they use their work to train AI systems.

subContents.title: Common ground:
subContents.text: No statements met the thresholds necessary to be considered as a point of common ground (at least 20 votes, and at least 70% agreement).

subContents.title: Differences of opinion:
subContents.text: No statements met the thresholds necessary to be considered as a significant difference of opinion (at least 20 votes, and more than 40%% and 60%% difference in agreement rate between groups).

subContents.title: ### Workforce impact of AI automation (4 statements)
subContents.text: This topic included 1 subtopic, comprising a total of 4 statements.

subContents.title: #### Job loss due to automation (2 statements)
subContents.text: This subtopic had high alignment compared to the other subtopics.

subContents.title: Prominent themes were:
subContents.text:
* **Job Loss Assistance**: Workers who lose their jobs because of AI should receive concrete support for new employment rather than merely guidance.  
* **Deployment Pace**: There is a need to pause AI implementation until its impact on individuals is fully understood.

subContents.title: Common ground:
subContents.text: No statements met the thresholds necessary to be considered as a point of common ground (at least 20 votes, and at least 70% agreement).

subContents.title: Differences of opinion:
subContents.text: No statements met the thresholds necessary to be considered as a significant difference of opinion (at least 20 votes, and more than 40%% and 60%% difference in agreement rate between groups).

subContents.title: ### Community involvement in AI governance (3 statements)
subContents.text: This topic included 1 subtopic, comprising a total of 3 statements.

subContents.title: #### Participatory Decision-Making (2 statements)
subContents.text: This subtopic had high alignment compared to the other subtopics.

subContents.title: Prominent themes were:
subContents.text:
* **Inclusive Decision‑Making for Dementia Care**: The statement asserts that people living with dementia should have a say in whether AI tools are used in their care.  
* **Community Voice in Local Institutions**: The statement claims communities should have a voice in deciding how AI is used in local schools and hospitals.  
* **Participatory Authority Over AI Use**: Both statements frame participation as essential to determining AI deployment in care and education settings.

subContents.title: Common ground:
subContents.text: No statements met the thresholds necessary to be considered as a point of common ground (at least 20 votes, and at least 70% agreement).

subContents.title: Differences of opinion:
subContents.text: No statements met the thresholds necessary to be considered as a significant difference of opinion (at least 20 votes, and more than 40%% and 60%% difference in agreement rate between groups).

comments.text:
AI in care homes should free up time for staff to spend with residents, not replace human
contact.
People living with dementia deserve a say in whether AI tools are used in their care.

Using AI to keep an elderly person company when no human is available is better than
leaving them alone.

Families caring for someone with a serious illness should be offered AI tools to help, even if
those tools are not perfect.
When AI helps make a decision about your benefits, housing, or health, you should be told.

topics.name: Transparency and accountability in AI decisions

comments.text:
If an AI system treats people unfairly because of their race, age, or disability, someone
should be held responsible.

topics.name: Transparency and accountability in AI decisions

comments.text:
People should always be able to reach a real person when dealing with a government
service, even if AI handles most tasks.

The UK should create an independent body with real power to shut down harmful AI
systems.

People are being harmed by AI-driven decisions while the government takes too long to
act.

topics.name: Transparency and accountability in AI decisions

comments.text:
Workers who lose their jobs because of AI should get real help finding new work, not just
advice.

Companies should not be allowed to replace workers with AI unless they help those workers
find new roles.

New AI data centres in Oxfordshire will create good jobs for local people, not just for tech
workers from elsewhere.

Communities should have a voice in deciding how AI is used in their local schools and
hospitals.
Schools should teach children to question what AI tells them, not just how to use it.

AI tools in schools do more to help struggling students catch up than they do to harm
learning.
When I talk to a chatbot or AI assistant, I should always be told it is not a real person.

topics.name: Transparency and accountability in AI decisions

comments.text:
Big technology companies care more about profits than about what happens to our
communities.

A community that takes care of its people matters more than one with the most advanced
technology.

AI companies should have to pay artists and writers when they use their work to train AI
systems.

topics.name: Transparency and accountability in AI decisions

comments.text: We should slow down on AI until we better understand what it does to people.
